deck_state.title: Impacts of Urban Green Infrastructure on Air Quality in Major Cities

slides.title: Impacts of Urban Green Infrastructure on Air Quality in Major Cities
Agenda
bullets: Pathways and Sources of Air Quality Impacts
Empirical Evidence on Air Quality Measurements
Health and Ecosystem Consequences of Air Quality Changes
Comparative Analysis
Policy and Mitigation Strategies
Evidence Gaps and Uncertainties

slides.title: Environmental Stakes of Urban Green Infrastructure on Air Quality
bullets: What does the current evidence reveal about urban green infrastructure, and why does it matter?
Evidence indicates that green and grey infrastructure interact with local meteorological conditions and traffic-related air pollution.
To what extent does urban green infrastructure influence air quality in major cities?
This deck focuses on pathways and sources of air quality impacts from urban green infrastructure.
Main argument: measured evidence provides a nuanced basis for targeted mitigation strategies. Source: nature.com, Evaluating green and grey urban infrastructure impacts on particulate pollution

slides.title: Pathways and Sources of Air Quality Impacts from Urban Green Infrastructure
bullets: A Dublin city-square case study examined evolving green and grey urban morphologies.
Parks are significant parts of urban landscapes and influence local air quality conditions.
Green and grey infrastructure effects vary with traffic, weather, and urban form.

slides.title: Empirical Evidence on Air Quality Measurements in Urban Green Spaces
bullets: The evidence is indirect and should be interpreted cautiously.
A monitoring campaign and ENVI-met modelling were used to assess current and future scenarios.
Urban parks contain substantial tree assets that may affect air quality outcomes.

slides.title: Health and Ecosystem Consequences of Air Quality Changes from Urban Green Infrastructure
bullets: The evidence gives this section indirect support.
Green roofs and tree barriers can improve air quality and reduce heat-island effects.
Green infrastructure can reduce particulate pollution and ground-level ozone.

slides.title: Comparative Analysis of Urban Green Infrastructure and Alternative Air Quality Interventions
bullets: The current evidence gives this section only indirect support, so treat it as a cautious interpretation.

slides.title: Policy and Mitigation Strategies for Managing Air Quality Impacts of Urban Green Infrastructure
bullets: The evidence gives this section indirect support.
Urban parks have substantial structural value.
Green infrastructure can improve runoff quality, reduce flooding, lower heat, and reduce building energy demand.

slides.title: Evidence Gaps and Uncertainties in Assessing Air Quality Impacts of Urban Green Infrastructure
bullets: The evidence gives this section indirect support.
Effects of parks and open space vary by city scale, parkland amount, and tree cover.
Heat islands emerge when natural land cover is replaced by pavement, buildings, and other heat-retaining surfaces.

slides.title: Synthesis and Recommendations for Future Urban Green Infrastructure Planning
bullets: Green and grey infrastructure interact with weather and traffic-related air pollution.
The Dublin case examined local urban development and park growth effects.
Limitation: when measured effects are unavailable, frame health and ecosystem effects as open questions.
Outlook: further evidence would sharpen these conclusions.

user_request: Please rewrite only the bullet text on slide 3 more clearly, but keep the nature.com citation about particulate pollution. Do not change the slide title, layout, or any other slide.

deck_state.title: Machine Learning in Clinical Risk Prediction

slides.title: Machine Learning in Clinical Risk Prediction
Agenda
bullets: Clinical motivation
Model development
Validation results
Deployment risks
Open questions

slides.title: Why Risk Prediction Matters
bullets: Risk scores support triage and resource allocation.
Poorly calibrated models can worsen clinical decision-making.
Prospective validation is needed before deployment. Source: Clinical Prediction Model Review

slides.title: Model Development Workflow
bullets: Candidate predictors are selected from structured clinical records.
Missing data are handled before model fitting.
Performance is compared using discrimination and calibration metrics.

slides.title: Validation Results Across Cohorts
bullets: External validation checks whether performance transfers across settings.
Calibration drift can emerge when patient populations change.
Decision-curve analysis can show whether model use adds clinical value.

slides.title: Deployment Risks and Governance Needs
bullets: Bias monitoring is required after deployment.
Clinicians need clear explanations of model scope and limits.
Audit trails should preserve reviewability.

user_request: Give slide 6 a more precise title while keeping the bullet content unchanged.
